title: What makes Evangelion so endearing
wikitext:
[]: What makes Evangelion so endearing?             Quote Post by GAP » Thu Dec 24, 2009 10:50 pm No doubt Evangelion is a unique show but what makes a show like this so endearing? What makes a particular anime like this worthy of dissection out of all the other anime out there mecha or otherwise? Is it the amount of content, symbolism or jsut the characters?

[]:             Quote Post by GVman » Thu Dec 24, 2009 10:54 pm It's Pen^2. Without him, EVA would be nothing.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:02 pm It's me. I make Eva so endearing.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:07 pm Obviously, as Xard is fond of saying, "Dem boobs be good" and that is the main reason we all keep watching. Ok, serious answer: Initially I think it starts with a strong portrayal of the characters which a great many viewers can empathize strongly with combined with a story full of mystery and promise that goes very unexpected places. When it finishes it isn't auto-resolving and requires extra input from the viewer to really "get". So these viewers will come online and research and read and maybe discuss and write and think about it. The more they do this, the more they realize how much there is worth discussing and writing and thinking about. The mysteries seem to never resolve without opening up a whole new set of mysteries. It doesn't hurt that there are so many indelible and provocative moments that stick in people's memory and inspire them to think and write about it, discuss it, etc.

[]:             Quote Post by GasmaskAvenger » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:09 pm for me, its that it doesn't try to be things like "OMG TEH IS SOO BAHDAZZ!" & such the whole "We're all screwed-up & flawed. Not perfect, but flawed" is what does it to me. Having the entire cast of characters flawed, damaged people is what ultimately enduring of course, there's also the EVA/Angel fights & the massive dosages of mindfuck, but those are just the decorations

[]: Re: What makes Evangelion so endearing?             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:15 pm GAP wrote: Is it the amount of content, symbolism or jsut the characters?

[]:             Quote Post by Quiddity » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:47 pm Very good writing and design (not necessarily the animation itself, but the design of the Angels, Evas, etc...). Eva isn't that unique or original, but did manage to be better written than the majority of the other mecha animes out there.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Thu Dec 24, 2009 11:59 pm NGE is awesome because it pretended to give everyone what they thought they wanted, and then succinctly explained to them why they didn't really want that. ---------------- Quiddity wrote: Eva isn't that unique or original

[]:             Quote Post by AVman9 » Fri Dec 25, 2009 2:46 am For me it literally opened my eyes to myself. I realized that no one understands anyone else perfectly, and that everyone has some sort of major flaw. Plus it disturbed the hell out of me...which is pretty cool.

[]:             Quote Post by Uriel Septim VII » Fri Dec 25, 2009 3:17 am It's the only show more than ten episodes long where I can remember in detail how each episode plays, and I didn't watch the series sixty eight times (although I'm sure some of you have surpassed that claim).

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Dec 25, 2009 12:02 pm Essentially, it's incompleteness. It's precisely because Evangelion forces the viewer to piece together the details that we are still here discussing it. At the exact opposite end of this spectrum is something like Death Note. While it is indeed a fantastic production, Death Note is so airtight in its attention to detail that very little about it can be discussed beyond what-if scenarios and questioning subjective details such as character motivation.

[]:             Quote Post by alabaster » Fri Dec 25, 2009 7:34 pm I have to agree with most people here. Eva's open endedness and it's unanswered questions drive watchers to research and discuss and ponder. You can rewatch it (which I just did) and still have lots of questions. And there's no classic hero to sweep in and save the day. Instead there are a group of very flawed, very human and often unlikeable people working, all with different motivations, toward some goal. And they're not even sure what that goal is. The show has some very memorable moments as well and some striking visuals that stay with the viewer. And the themes of the show are very universal. Most people can relate to wanting to be loved, or loneliness.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Dec 25, 2009 8:21 pm Quiddity wrote: Eva isn't that unique or original,

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Fri Dec 25, 2009 10:27 pm I think eva is so endearing because the show is like a mirror for a lot of people. The interpretation of events can differ greatly depending on the viewer’s personal options to a greater degree then with most other shows.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Dec 26, 2009 6:46 am fanservice , obviously

[]:             Quote Post by Adekis » Sat Dec 26, 2009 3:56 pm Neon Genesis Evangelion doesn't give all the details, which causes people to try to piece it together themselves, but they're unlikely to get all the information in just one viewing, so they watch it multple times. People are trying to figure out what they think it means, which increases it's popularity. The fact that the characters are pretty much all interesting probably doesn't hurt. Neither does the fanservice. So, I just said what everone else has already said.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Sat Dec 26, 2009 4:16 pm the inspired style and philosophical undertones that permeate everything throughout Evangelion IMO, it is the best attempt to ape the French New Wave since the end of the New Hollywood and teh bewbz are a nice touch as well, if you know what I mean

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Dec 26, 2009 4:20 pm For me, it's the characters, Shinji especially. The way their problems are touched upon and examined really hits home with me, I guess.

[]:             Quote Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Sat Dec 26, 2009 4:36 pm it's the entire equation, the characters, the setting, the situation(s), the philosophy, the phsycology, every facet of it has helped it become not just a simple anime, but a phenomenon know on both sides of the globe. Sailor Star Dust wrote: For me, it's the characters, Shinji especially. The way their problems are touched upon and examined really hits home with me, I guess.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sat Dec 26, 2009 5:50 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Essentially, it's incompleteness. It's precisely because Evangelion forces the viewer to piece together the details that we are still here discussing it.
